metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify which epic/story owns 'story E read layer', add it to the sibling roster if it belongs to this epic, OR pin its interface contract (module path, public API) in both children's scope sections so each child codes against the same specified surface.
findings.finding: Both children reference a 'story E read layer' as the runtime data-access interface consumed by the server routes (child A: 'one index route using the read layer from story E'; child B scope: 'add the /ticket/<id> route consuming the story-E read layer'), but story E appears in neither the sibling roster nor either child's own scope as a deliverable. No sibling owns the delivery or specification of this read layer within the epic, creating a scope-owned-by-none gap.
findings.location: child a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c (Scope / What) and child ff6f-b655-4058-4308 (Scope)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a third child story to this epic that owns the read-layer module (data access abstraction over Epic 1's sidecar files), with both A and B depending on it; or (b) if story E is an Epic 1 deliverable, name it explicitly in the parent's data contract table and add an import/interface stub to child A's scope so both children have a concrete contract to code against.

findings.finding: Child a3d7 registers the `audit` CLI intercept as dispatching both `serve` AND `show` subcommands, but neither child defines, scopes, or specifies AC for the `show` subcommand. It is referenced but owned by no sibling.
findings.suggested_fix: If `show` is intentionally deferred, replace the dispatch with a 'not yet implemented' SystemExit (matching the disabled-path pattern) and document it as a future story. If it is in scope, add its specification to the appropriate child.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the data contract exactly where and how `evidence` and `scenarios` will be persisted by Epic 1 story A (e.g., added to each finding object in `_slim`, overriding the current exclusion), with a note on why the exclusion rationale no longer applies.
findings.finding: The data contract table requires `evidence` and `scenarios` as fields for the 'Finding-provenance' UI region (sourced from plan-review v2 story A / code-review v2 story B), but the current plan-review `_slim` explicitly excludes them with the comment 'deliberately exclude runtime-only carriers (e.g. scenarios, evidence)'. Epic 1 story A must override this exclusion decision, but the plan gives no specification of how (add to `_slim`, a separate sidecar section, a second payload key) — leaving story A's scope ambiguous.
findings.location: Data contract table — 'Finding-provenance (per finding)' row; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py _slim comment (line 313)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact wire format for `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` in the Epic 1 data contract (e.g., per-finding keys in `_slim`/sidecar payload: `finding.block_threshold: float`, `finding.blocking_enabled: bool`) so stories A, B, and the UI story share one unambiguous schema.
findings.finding: The data contract table lists `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` as required per-finding fields for the Threshold Meter UI region, to be delivered by Epic 1 stories A/B — but neither field is present in the current plan-review `_slim` (sidecar.py lines 308–351) nor in the code-review sidecar payload. The plan never specifies the wire format these fields must take when Epic 1 adds them: field on the finding object itself, a per-criterion lookup table, or a parallel array. Without this specification, story A/B implementers cannot produce a schema the UI story can consume.
findings.location: Data contract table — 'Threshold meter (per finding)' row; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py _slim (lines 308–351); src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py build_payload (lines 85–101)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the data contract: 'Stories A and B persist `block_threshold` (float) and `blocking_enabled` (bool) as keys directly on each finding object in the sidecar (alongside `decision`, `priority`, etc.) — the UI reads `finding["block_threshold"]` and `finding["blocking_enabled"]` from every finding row in the sidecar payload.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rendering specification: define what each UI panel displays when its Epic 1 source fields are absent from a sidecar (e.g., 'panel shows a "data not available for this round" notice when the required fields are missing'), covering at minimum the Threshold Meter (missing block_threshold), Finding-provenance (missing evidence/scenarios), and Completion panel (missing criteria[]).
findings.finding: The plan states the UI 'MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed', but specifies no fallback rendering protocol for the case where a ticket's sidecar predates Epic 1 landing (i.e., old `plan_review_result_v1` sidecars without `block_threshold`/`evidence`/`scenarios`, or old completion events without `criteria[]`). There is no specified degraded-render behaviour (e.g., 'show placeholder', 'hide panel', 'display warning') when the required fields are absent from a stored sidecar.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Data contract (the named interface Epic 1 must expose)' — 'This epic MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed'; Acceptance Criteria — 'With the feature enabled, `rebar audit serve` serves a ticket audit page showing...the completion `criteria[]` rows'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen child B AC-7's oracle to explicitly require seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds on one ticket and asserting the older round's distinct content is rendered for EACH gate independently.
findings.finding: Child B's round-history oracle (B-AC7) does not explicitly require ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds to be seeded and each older round to be selected — it says 'seeding ≥2 rounds' without mandating both gate types have multi-round history exercised. The parent AC-2 oracle is explicit: '≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds on one ticket, selecting the older round in EACH gate.' A test seeding ≥2 rounds of only one gate type would satisfy B-AC7 but fail the parent criterion.
findings.location: child ff6f-b655-4058-4308, Acceptance Criteria — AC7 (round selector oracle)
findings.suggested_fix: Revise B-AC7 oracle to: 'an E2E test seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds on one ticket; asserting the plan-review round selector shows its N, defaults to latest, and requesting its older round renders its (distinct-from-latest) findings; AND asserting the same for the code-review round selector independently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the novel architectural decision (optional loopback web server as a lazy-imported CLI extra, static-export rejection rationale, loopback-only binding) in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update referenced from the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — adding an optional FastAPI+Jinja2 web server as a lazy-imported extra to a CLI tool, with a loopback-only binding and an explicit rejection of static HTML export — but nowhere commits to capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Compatibility & rollback section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a story task (or acceptance criterion) requiring an ADR entry or CLAUDE.md section documenting: (1) why FastAPI+Jinja2 over static export, (2) the loopback-only binding rationale, (3) the lazy-import pattern constraints for future optional extras.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly how the target ticket is identified in `rebar audit serve` (e.g., `rebar audit serve <ticket-id>` positional argument, or `--ticket <id>` flag) so the command interface is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `rebar audit serve` command's ticket-identification argument is unspecified. An executor cannot implement the command entrypoint without knowing HOW the target ticket is supplied (e.g., positional `<ticket-id>` argument, `--ticket` flag, auto-detect from current working directory/git branch, or similar). This is a real design decision deferred to the implementer, not an obvious convention.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'A `rebar audit serve` subcommand starts a read-only web server…'; Acceptance Criteria — third bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line under the Scope or Success Criteria section: e.g., 'The subcommand accepts a required positional argument `<ticket-id>` (the ticket's canonical identifier as stored by Epic 1) and derives all data paths from it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider whether the scaffolding/page split delivers independent auditor value at each child boundary, or collapse into a single story that ships the minimal end-to-end slice (server + one rendered panel) so at least one child closes with observable auditor value.
findings.finding: The two child stories split the feature horizontally by layer (server scaffolding vs. page rendering), meaning neither child alone delivers any auditor value — an empty server with no meaningful page (child a) and a renderer with no server (child b) are each individually worthless to the auditor persona. The rubric explicitly flags horizontal-by-layer splitting as an anti-pattern and requires each decomposed piece to be independently valuable and releasable.
findings.location: Decomposition state — children cultivated-aquatic-crow (scaffolding) and depressant-amethyst-wasp (page rendering)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the [ui] extra dependency list to explicitly include `jinja2` (e.g. `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]`), since the existing `reviewbot` extra does not include it and FastAPI's template rendering requires it as a separate package.
findings.finding: The plan states the [ui] extra 'REUSES the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack', but the reviewbot extra in pyproject.toml contains only fastapi, uvicorn[standard], and boto3 — Jinja2 is absent. FastAPI's server-side template rendering (fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates) requires Jinja2 as an explicit dependency. An implementer who takes the plan's claim at face value when writing the [ui] extra declaration will omit Jinja2, and template rendering will fail at runtime with a missing-dependency ImportError.
findings.location: Approach rationale section — 'REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack'
findings.suggested_fix: In the [ui] extra declaration (pyproject.toml), list jinja2 explicitly: `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]`. Update the 'Approach rationale' wording to 'reuses FastAPI (already in the reviewbot extra) and adds Jinja2 for templating' to prevent the same misread in child stories.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and explicitly name `jinja2` (or `Jinja2`) as a required pip dependency in the planned `nava-rebar[ui]` extra, since it is NOT in the existing `reviewbot` extra and is required for `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` to function.
findings.finding: The plan asserts it will 'REUSE the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack' for server-rendered Jinja2 templates, but the existing `reviewbot` extra (`fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, `boto3`) does NOT include `jinja2`. FastAPI's Jinja2Templates support (via starlette.templating) requires `jinja2` as a separate install — without it, importing `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` raises an ImportError at runtime. The codebase has no prior Jinja2 usage at all (zero hits in a repo-wide search). The plan's new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra never lists its pip dependencies, leaving the `jinja2` requirement unverified.
findings.location: Approach rationale section: 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach rationale and/or Scope section, add: 'The `[ui]` extra will include `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `jinja2` (net-new; not in the existing `reviewbot` extra — FastAPI's Jinja2Templates support requires it as a separate install).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion (with oracle test) asserting that `rebar audit serve` hard-codes or validates a loopback-only bind address (127.0.0.1 / ::1), or alternatively add an explicit AC requiring HTTP authentication when any non-loopback interface is configured.
findings.finding: The loopback-only binding that justifies the no-auth stance is stated only in Approach Rationale prose and is not enforced by any Acceptance Criterion or oracle test. No AC requires that the server hard-codes or validates a loopback-only bind address, so a future `--host` flag or config option could silently expose an unauthenticated service on a non-loopback interface without violating any AC.
findings.location: Approach Rationale ('bound to loopback only'); Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding oracle)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'The server MUST bind exclusively to 127.0.0.1 (IPv4) and/or ::1 (IPv6); any attempt to bind to a non-loopback address is rejected with a clear error. Oracle: a test asserting the listening socket address is loopback-only and that passing --host 0.0.0.0 raises a startup error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `jinja2` (or `Jinja2`) as a required pip dependency in the new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra's definition, and correct the 'REUSING the reviewbot extra' rationale to 'extending the reviewbot stack pattern with the addition of jinja2' to prevent implementors from omitting it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack,' but the reviewbot extra (`fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, `boto3`) does NOT include `jinja2`. FastAPI's Jinja2Templates (via starlette.templating) requires `jinja2` as a separate pip package — without it, any template-render call raises ImportError. The repo has zero prior jinja2 usage (no source hits), making this a net-new dependency. The new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra is named but its pip dependency list is never specified in the plan, leaving jinja2 unaccounted for.
findings.location: Approach rationale section: 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack rather than adding a second web framework'; Compatibility & rollback: 'its web deps live in a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Approach rationale, replace 'REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack' with 'following the `reviewbot` extra pattern, adding `jinja2` as the one new dependency needed for Jinja2Templates.' In the Scope or Compatibility section, enumerate the new `[ui]` extra's deps: `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]`.

findings.finding: AC2's stated oracle only seeds and verifies plan-review history ('seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds, selecting the older round') but the criterion text it is meant to satisfy covers BOTH plan- and code-review history navigation, leaving code-review round selection unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC2 oracle: 'a live end-to-end test seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds, selecting the older round of each type, and asserting its distinct content renders.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
